chunk_summary: This section outlines a stepwise approach to managing enteral feed intolerance, focusing on vomiting, diarrhea, and underdelivery in critically ill patients, while prioritizing aspiration avoidance, hydration, and energy balance.
learning_objectives: Understand the stepwise management of vomiting and diarrhea in enteral feed intolerance.
Identify key factors contributing to feed underdelivery and strategies to address them.
Learn the importance of avoiding routine gastric residual volume (GRV) checks in critically ill children.
Recognize when to escalate to postpyloric feeding in cases of recurrent vomiting.
key_facts: Routine GRV checks are not recommended in critically ill children as they increase interruptions and underfeeding without clear outcome benefits.
If feed delivery is below 80% of the prescribed volume for 24–48 hours, it should be treated as a clinical problem.
For vomiting, reassess tube position, reduce bolus size, extend delivery time, or switch to continuous feeding while maintaining daily targets.
Diarrhea management includes slowing feed rate, confirming mixing accuracy, reviewing drug excipients, and avoiding reflex switching to lactose-free formulas.
Recurrent vomiting with desaturation or feed intolerance may require escalation to postpyloric feeding.
reference_quote: In critically ill children, do not rely on routine GRV checks as the primary tolerance metric, because this practice increases interruptions and underfeeding without clear outcome benefit. (ASPEN/SCCM 2017)
document_key_topics: Stepwise response to vomiting and diarrhea
